questions.prompt: Which method binds this without immediately calling the function?
questions.explanation: bind() returns a new function with this permanently set. call() and apply() invoke immediately.

questions.prompt: Arrow functions: can you change their this with .call()?
options: No — arrow function this is baked in at creation and cannot be overridden
Only if the arrow function is at module scope
questions.explanation: Arrow functions have no own this. Passing a different thisArg to .call(), .apply(), or .bind() has no effect.

questions.prompt: const obj = { name: 'Alice', greet: () => console.log(this.name) }; What does obj.greet() print?
questions.explanation: The arrow function captures this from its definition scope (module scope), where this is undefined (or globalThis.name in global scope, likely undefined).

questions.prompt: What is the highest-priority this-binding rule?
questions.explanation: Priority order: new > explicit (.call/.bind/.apply) > implicit (obj.method()) > default.

questions.prompt: Give TWO ways to pass an object method as a callback while preserving its this.
call() vs apply() — what is the difference?
options: call takes args as array; apply takes args individually
apply takes args as array; call takes args individually
call returns a new function; apply invokes immediately
There is no difference in modern JavaScript
questions.explanation: fn.call(thisArg, arg1, arg2) — individual args. fn.apply(thisArg, [arg1, arg2]) — array of args.

questions.prompt: What is the output order? console.log('A'); setTimeout(()=>console.log('C'),0); Promise.resolve().then(()=>console.log('B'));
questions.explanation: A is synchronous. B is a microtask (Promise.then) — runs before the next macrotask. C is a macrotask (setTimeout).

questions.prompt: Promise.all vs Promise.allSettled: when does Promise.all reject?
options: Never — it always waits for all
questions.explanation: Promise.all rejects immediately when any promise rejects. Promise.allSettled waits for all and never rejects.

questions.prompt: Why is `arr.forEach(async item => { await process(item) })` wrong for sequential processing?
options: forEach doesn't await the async callbacks — all run in parallel without waiting
forEach returns a Promise that must be awaited
await is not valid inside forEach
questions.explanation: forEach ignores returned Promises. It fires all async callbacks concurrently and moves on. Use for...of with await for sequential execution.

questions.prompt: How do you run two async operations in parallel with async/await?
options: const a = await fn1(); const b = await fn2();
They always run in parallel automatically
questions.explanation: Awaiting sequentially (option A) runs them in series. Promise.all starts both simultaneously and awaits both results.

questions.prompt: What does the event loop do between each macrotask?
questions.explanation: After each macrotask completes, the event loop runs ALL pending microtasks before picking up the next macrotask.

questions.prompt: Write an async function that fetches a user and their posts in parallel and returns both.
An async function always returns:
questions.explanation: async functions always return a Promise, even if you return a plain value or never explicitly return.

questions.prompt: What does 'type': 'module' in package.json do?
options: Forces all files to use TypeScript
Makes .js files treated as ES modules
Loads the types field from package exports
questions.explanation: "type": "module" tells Node.js to treat .js files as ESM. Without it, .js files default to CommonJS.

questions.prompt: A CommonJS module needs to use a pure ESM package. What is the correct approach?
options: Use require() — it automatically handles ESM
Use dynamic import(): const mod = await import('pkg')
Downgrade the package to its CommonJS version
questions.explanation: Dynamic import() works in CJS and returns a Promise. It is the only way to load a pure ESM module from CJS.

questions.prompt: What is the most critical tsconfig setting for TypeScript strictness?
questions.explanation: strict: true enables all strict checks at once. It's the master flag — don't enable them piecemeal.

questions.prompt: When should you set noEmit: true in tsconfig?
options: When you only want to generate .d.ts files
When a bundler handles transpilation and tsc is only for type checking
questions.explanation: When Vite, Webpack, or esbuild compiles TypeScript, tsc's job is type checking only. noEmit prevents it from generating JS files.

questions.prompt: ESM has tree-shaking advantages over CJS because:
options: ESM imports/exports are static and analyzable at build time
CJS is not supported by modern bundlers
ESM modules are smaller by default
questions.explanation: ESM imports are static — the shape of exports is known at parse time. Bundlers can determine which exports are unused and eliminate them.

questions.prompt: With module: NodeNext, what extension must you use in import statements for TypeScript files?
questions.answer_key: .js — e.g., import { foo } from './bar.js'. TypeScript resolves bar.js to bar.ts. This mirrors Node.js ESM's requirement for explicit extensions.

questions.prompt: Type erasure means:
options: Types are converted to runtime checks
TypeScript removes all type info before the output runs
Generics are replaced with any at runtime
questions.explanation: All TypeScript annotations, interfaces, and generics are stripped. The compiled output is pure JavaScript with no type information.

questions.prompt: const greeting = 'hello'; — what is TypeScript's inferred type?
questions.explanation: const narrows inference to the literal type 'hello' because the value cannot change.

questions.prompt: Which utility handles null AND undefined together?
questions.explanation: NonNullable<T> = Exclude<T, null | undefined>. It removes both null and undefined from a type.

questions.prompt: interface vs type — which supports declaration merging?
questions.explanation: Interfaces can be declared multiple times and TypeScript merges them. Type aliases cannot be redeclared.

questions.prompt: function f(x?: string) — what is the type of x inside the function?
questions.explanation: Optional parameters become T | undefined. x is either a string or undefined (if not passed).

questions.prompt: When should you add explicit type annotations vs relying on TypeScript inference?
questions.answer_key: Always annotate function parameters (TypeScript cannot infer them). Optionally annotate public function return types for documentation. Skip annotations for variable initializations with obvious literals and function returns in simple cases — inference handles these well.

questions.prompt: Why is `as User` on JSON.parse() output dangerous?
options: TypeScript does not allow as on JSON.parse
It silences type checking with no runtime validation
JSON.parse already returns the correct type
questions.explanation: as User tells TypeScript to trust you without checking. If the data has wrong shape, you'll get runtime errors TypeScript couldn't catch.

questions.prompt: function identity<T>(value: T): T — the T here is called:
questions.explanation: T is a generic type parameter — a placeholder that gets filled with a real type when the function is called.

questions.prompt: Partial<User> produces a type where:
questions.explanation: Partial<T> makes every property in T optional (adds ? to each key).

questions.prompt: A discriminated union requires:
options: All members to have the same properties
Each member to have a shared literal-typed discriminant property
The union to be created with the & operator
All members to extend a base interface
questions.explanation: The discriminant is a shared property with a unique literal type per member (like status: 'loading' | 'success' | 'error').

questions.prompt: Omit<User, 'id' | 'createdAt'> produces:
options: Only the id and createdAt fields
User without the id and createdAt fields
The type of id and createdAt combined
User where id and createdAt are optional
questions.explanation: Omit<T, K> creates a new type with all properties of T except those in K.

questions.prompt: How do you make TypeScript error if a switch/case doesn't handle all union members?
options: In the default case, assign the value to a never-typed variable
TypeScript does this automatically with union types
questions.explanation: default: const _: never = value; — if a union member is unhandled, TypeScript errors because it's assignable to the type, not never.

questions.prompt: What is a type predicate and how is it different from a plain boolean check?
questions.answer_key: A type predicate is a return type annotation 'val is User' on a function. It tells TypeScript to narrow val's type in the if-true branch. A plain boolean check doesn't affect TypeScript's type narrowing — it's just a runtime condition.

questions.prompt: What does <T extends { name: string }> mean?
options: T must be a subclass of { name: string }
T is constrained to only types that have a name: string property
T is replaced by { name: string } at compile time
questions.explanation: extends in a generic constraint limits the allowed types to those that satisfy the given shape (structural typing).

questions.prompt: What is the security risk of setting innerHTML with user-controlled data?
options: XSS — the browser executes injected script tags
questions.explanation: innerHTML parses HTML including <script> tags. User input set via innerHTML can execute arbitrary JavaScript (XSS). Use textContent instead.

questions.prompt: document.querySelector<HTMLInputElement>('#email') — the generic parameter does what?
options: Tells TypeScript the return type is HTMLInputElement instead of Element
Filters only input elements in the DOM
It has no effect — it's just documentation
questions.explanation: The generic parameter gives TypeScript the specific type, granting access to input-specific properties like .value without casting.

questions.prompt: How does event delegation work?
options: Each element has one listener delegated to the browser
One listener on a parent handles events from descendant children via bubbling
Listeners are shared between sibling elements
The browser delegates event handling to Web Workers
questions.explanation: Events bubble up to parent elements. Attaching one listener to a container handles clicks on all descendants, including dynamically added ones.

questions.prompt: element.removeEventListener('click', handler) — when does this work?
options: Always, for any click handler on the element
Only when handler is the exact same function reference as the one added
Only for the first registered listener
questions.explanation: removeEventListener requires an identical function reference. Anonymous inline functions () => {} cannot be removed.

questions.prompt: element.closest('.container') does what?
options: Returns the nearest ancestor (including self) matching .container
Returns all .container elements in the document
questions.explanation: closest() traverses ancestors upward from the element, returning the first match (or null).

questions.prompt: Write event delegation code on a #todo-list ul that logs the text content of any clicked li.
Why shouldn't you use readFileSync in a production HTTP server?
options: It blocks the event loop until the file is read — no other requests are served
It only works in CJS modules
questions.explanation: Synchronous I/O blocks the event loop. While reading, all other connections wait. Use fs/promises or callbacks instead.

questions.prompt: process.env.API_KEY in TypeScript has type:
questions.explanation: TypeScript types all process.env values as string | undefined because they may not be set.

questions.prompt: How do you get __dirname in an ES module Node.js file?
options: __dirname is available in ESM just like in CJS
Derive it from import.meta.url using fileURLToPath and path.dirname
questions.explanation: __dirname doesn't exist in ESM. You derive it: const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))

questions.prompt: In a raw Node.js HTTP server, how does the request body arrive?
options: As a parsed object in req.body
As a stream of chunks via 'data' events
questions.explanation: The body is a readable stream. You must collect 'data' events and assemble the full body, then parse it.

questions.prompt: What happens if you never call res.end() in a Node.js HTTP handler?
options: Node.js automatically closes the response after 30 seconds
The connection hangs until the client times out
The response sends with empty body automatically
questions.explanation: You must always call res.end(). Failing to do so keeps the connection open until the client or server times out.

questions.prompt: Write a Node.js HTTP handler that returns a 200 JSON response { ok: true }.
questions.answer_key:
res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' });
res.end(JSON.stringify({ ok: true }));

questions.prompt: Which signal should a Node.js server listen for to handle graceful shutdown in a container?
questions.explanation: Containers send SIGTERM before stopping. SIGKILL cannot be caught. Handling SIGTERM lets you close the server cleanly.

M01.title: M01 Mastery Check — The JavaScript Landscape
M01.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: Name the three layers that make up the JavaScript execution environment.
questions.answer_key: ECMAScript spec, JavaScript engine (e.g. V8), host environment (browser or Node.js)

questions.prompt: What command do you run in CI to type-check a TypeScript project without emitting files?
questions.answer_key: tsc --noEmit

questions.prompt: TypeScript interfaces exist at runtime and can be used to validate incoming data.
questions.explanation: Type erasure removes all TypeScript constructs. Nothing type-related survives to runtime.

M02.title: M02 Mastery Check — Variables, Types & Coercion
M02.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: Fix the bug: for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0) } — it prints 3,3,3.
questions.answer_key: Change var to let. let creates a new binding per iteration, so each closure captures its own i.

questions.prompt: List all 6 falsy values in JavaScript.
questions.answer_key: false, 0, 0n, '' (empty string), null, undefined — also NaN

questions.prompt: const person = { name: 'Alice' }; person.name = 'Bob'; — This throws a TypeError.
questions.explanation: const prevents rebinding the variable. Mutating properties of the object is allowed.

questions.prompt: An API returns price as the string '42.5'. How do you safely add 10 to it?
questions.answer_key: parseFloat('42.5') + 10 or Number('42.5') + 10 — either converts to number first. Do not use '42.5' + 10 which gives '42.510'.

M03.title: M03 Mastery Check — Collections & Iteration
M03.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: Write a one-liner: given an array of products, return the total price of all products where inStock is true.
questions.answer_key: products.filter(p => p.inStock).reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.price, 0)

questions.prompt: for...in is safe to use on arrays.
questions.explanation: for...in iterates over property names as strings ('0', '1', '2') and may also iterate inherited properties. Use for...of for arrays.

questions.prompt: What method creates a proper deep clone of a nested object in modern JavaScript?
questions.answer_key: structuredClone(obj)

questions.prompt: How do you remove a specific key from an object without mutating it?
questions.answer_key: const { keyToRemove, ...rest } = original; — rest is a new object without that key.

M04.title: M04 Mastery Check — Functions, Scope & Closures
M04.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: Write a makeAdder(n) factory function that returns a function adding n to any number.
An outer function can access variables declared inside a nested inner function.
questions.explanation: Scope chains flow outward only. Inner functions can access outer scope, but not the reverse.

questions.prompt: Which function type is fully hoisted and callable before its declaration line?
questions.answer_key: Function declarations — function foo() {}. NOT function expressions or arrow functions.

questions.prompt: Explain in one sentence what a closure is.
questions.answer_key: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its lexical scope even after that scope has exited.

M05.title: M05 Mastery Check — this Binding
M05.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: const go = obj.run; go(); — what is this inside go in strict mode?
questions.answer_key: undefined — implicit binding is lost when the method is extracted from the object.

questions.prompt: List the 5 this-binding rules in priority order (highest first).
questions.answer_key: 1. new binding, 2. Explicit binding (call/apply/bind), 3. Implicit binding (obj.method()), 4. Default binding, 5. Arrow (lexical, cannot be overridden)

questions.prompt: fn.bind(obj) calls fn immediately and returns the result.
questions.explanation: bind() returns a new function with this permanently set but does NOT invoke it. call() and apply() invoke immediately.

questions.prompt: Give two ways to pass a class method as a callback while keeping the correct this.
questions.answer_key: 1. this.method.bind(this), 2. () => this.method(), 3. Define as arrow class property: method = () => {}

M06.title: M06 Mastery Check — Async JavaScript
M06.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: What prints first: a Promise.then callback or a setTimeout(0) callback? Why?
questions.answer_key: Promise.then — it's a microtask. Microtasks drain after each synchronous task, before any macrotask (setTimeout).

questions.prompt: Fix: items.forEach(async item => { await save(item); }) — processing should be sequential.
questions.answer_key: for (const item of items) { await save(item); } — for...of respects await. forEach ignores returned Promises.

questions.prompt: An async function that returns 42 actually returns Promise<number>.
questions.explanation: async functions always return a Promise. The 42 is wrapped: Promise.resolve(42).

questions.prompt: You need to fetch user data and user settings at the same time. Write the call.
questions.answer_key: const [user, settings] = await Promise.all([fetchUser(id), fetchSettings(id)]);

M07.title: M07 Mastery Check — Modules & Tooling
M07.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: How does Node.js know if a .js file is ESM or CommonJS?
questions.answer_key: The 'type' field in package.json: 'module' = ESM, absent or 'commonjs' = CJS. Also: .mjs always ESM, .cjs always CJS.

questions.prompt: You can require() a pure ESM module from a CommonJS module.
questions.explanation: require() is synchronous and cannot load ESM. Use dynamic import() which returns a Promise.

questions.prompt: Name the two most critical tsconfig fields for a strict TypeScript project.
questions.answer_key: strict: true (enables all type safety checks) and noEmit: true (when using a bundler, keeps tsc for type checking only)

questions.prompt: What does the tsconfig 'target' field control?
questions.answer_key: The JavaScript syntax version in the output files. E.g., target: 'ES2022' means tsc outputs ES2022 syntax (const, arrow functions, etc.).

M08.title: M08 Mastery Check — TypeScript Foundations
M08.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: TypeScript type annotations can be used at runtime to validate data from an API.
questions.explanation: Type erasure removes all annotations. At runtime there are no types. Use Zod or a type guard for runtime validation.

questions.prompt: Model a function that takes a userId (number) and returns Promise<User>.
questions.answer_key: async function getUser(userId: number): Promise<User> { ... }

questions.prompt: When should you prefer interface over type alias?
questions.answer_key: For object shapes that need to be extended with extends or that benefit from declaration merging. Use type for unions, intersections, and utility type expressions.

questions.prompt: What is the difference in inference between: const x = 'hello' and let x = 'hello'?
questions.answer_key: const infers the literal type 'hello'. let infers the widened type string (since it can be reassigned).

M09.title: M09 Mastery Check — TypeScript Advanced
M09.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: Create a type for a PATCH /users/:id endpoint body — all User fields optional.
questions.answer_key: type UserPatch = Partial<User>;

questions.prompt: Design a discriminated union for a network request state with loading/success/error states.
questions.answer_key: type State<T> = { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; data: T } | { status: 'error'; message: string };

questions.prompt: ReturnType<typeof fn> extracts the return type of fn at compile time.
questions.explanation: ReturnType<F> uses TypeScript's infer keyword under the hood to extract the return type of function type F.

questions.prompt: How do you make TypeScript error if a new shape is added to a discriminated union but not handled in a switch?
questions.answer_key: Add default: const _: never = shape; — if any unhandled member reaches default, it's not assignable to never, causing a compile error.

M10.title: M10 Mastery Check — DOM & Browser APIs
M10.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: Setting element.textContent with user input is safe from XSS.
questions.explanation: textContent sets plain text — HTML is escaped. Unlike innerHTML, no script tags or HTML entities are parsed.

questions.prompt: Write code that toggles class 'open' on a button click.
questions.answer_key: button.addEventListener('click', () => { button.classList.toggle('open'); });

questions.prompt: Explain event delegation in one sentence and give one advantage.
questions.answer_key: Event delegation attaches one listener to a parent to handle events on children via bubbling. Advantage: fewer listeners, works for dynamically added children.

questions.prompt: Why can't you removeEventListener with an inline arrow function?
questions.answer_key: removeEventListener requires the exact same function reference as addEventListener. A new inline () => {} is a different reference each time.

M11.title: M11 Mastery Check — Node.js Foundations
M11.description: Quick applied check. No looking things up. Answer from memory.

questions.prompt: readFileSync() is appropriate in a high-traffic HTTP server.
questions.explanation: readFileSync blocks the event loop. Use fs/promises readFile or a callback-based approach in servers.

questions.prompt: Read a file 'config.json' and parse it as JSON using async/await.
What happens if you forget to call res.end() in a Node.js HTTP handler?
questions.answer_key: The connection hangs until the client times out. Always call res.end() in every code path.

questions.prompt: How do you get __dirname in a Node.js ESM file?

challenges.prompt: Write a function `improvedTypeof(value)` that returns a more accurate type label than the built-in `typeof` operator. It should return `'null'` for `null` (not `'object'`), `'array'` for arrays (not `'object'`), and the standard `typeof` result for everything else.

Write a function `formatJsVersion(edition)` that converts an ECMAScript edition number or year into the canonical version name.

- Edition 1–5 → `'ES1'` through `'ES5'`
- Edition 6 → `'ES2015'`, edition 7 → `'ES2016'`, and so on (add 2009 to editions 6+)
- Year 2015 or higher → `'ES2015'`, `'ES2016'`, etc. (pass through as-is)

Examples: `5` → `'ES5'`, `6` → `'ES2015'`, `2022` → `'ES2022'`

Write a function `safeParse(value)` that takes a string and converts it to a number. If the result is `NaN`, return `0` instead. Use `Number()` for conversion.

Note: `Number('')` returns `0` (not NaN), and `Number('abc')` returns `NaN`.

Write a function `strictEquals(pair)` that takes an array of two values and returns `true` if they are strictly equal (`===`), or `false` otherwise.

Key cases to handle: `1 === '1'` is `false` (different types), `NaN === NaN` is `false` (NaN is never equal to itself), `null === undefined` is `false`.

test_cases.name: Number and string are not equal
NaN is never equal to itself
null and undefined are not strictly equal
0 and false are not strictly equal

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `truthyCount(values)` that takes an array and returns the count of truthy values.

Remember JavaScript's falsy values: `false`, `0`, `''`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`. Everything else is truthy — including `[]`, `{}`, and `'0'`.

test_cases.name: Empty array and empty object are truthy

challenges.prompt: Write a function `sumPositives(numbers)` that takes an array of numbers and returns the sum of all values strictly greater than zero. Use `filter` and `reduce`.

Write a function `flatDedup(arrays)` that takes an array of arrays, flattens it one level, removes duplicate numbers, and returns the result sorted in ascending order.

Use `Array.flat()`, `Set` for deduplication, and `Array.sort()` with a numeric comparator.

test_cases.name: Larger spread with negatives sorted correctly

challenges.prompt: Write a function `extractValues(configs)` that takes an array of `{ key, value }` objects and returns an array of `value` strings where the `key` starts with `'user_'`. Preserve the original order.

test_cases.name: Filters user_ keys and returns values in order
Single user_ key returns its value
Interleaved user_ and non-user_ keys in order

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `repeatString(config)` that takes an object `{ str, times }` and returns `str` repeated `times` times. Use `String.prototype.repeat()`.

If `times` is 0, return an empty string.

Write a function `parsePathParts(config)` that takes an object `{ path, depth }` where `path` is a dot-separated string (e.g. `'user.address.city'`) and `depth` is a zero-based index. Return the segment at that depth.

This exercises property access and lexical scope: the function creates a local variable for the split result, then accesses it by index.

Write a function `runningTotal(amounts)` that takes an array of numbers and returns an array where each element is the running (cumulative) sum up to that index.

This is the classic closure pattern: use a variable declared outside the inner function to accumulate state across `map` iterations.

Example: `[10, 5, -3, 2]` → `[10, 15, 12, 14]`

test_cases.name: Zeroes then one gives running sums

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `applyWithContext(config)` that takes an object `{ values, op, operand }` and applies an arithmetic operation to each element in `values`.

Supported operations (apply `operand` to each value):
- `'add'`: `value + operand`
- `'multiply'`: `value * operand`
- `'subtract'`: `value - operand`

Return the results as an array (String() on an array joins with commas).

This simulates binding a context value (`operand`) to a method and mapping it over a collection.

Write a function `predictThis(scenario)` that takes a scenario name string and returns what `this` would be in that context.

Mapping:
- `'method-call'` → `'object'` (called as `obj.method()`)
- `'plain-function'` → `'undefined'` (strict mode default binding)
- `'arrow-function'` → `'outer-scope'` (lexical this from enclosing context)
- `'new-call'` → `'new-instance'` (called with `new`)
- `'explicit-bind'` → `'bound-object'` (called via `.call()`, `.apply()`, or `.bind()`)
- any other string → `'unknown'`

test_cases.name: Method call binds to the object
Plain function call in strict mode is undefined
new call binds to the new instance

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `processResults(results)` that takes an array of settled promise-like objects, each with `{ status: 'fulfilled' | 'rejected', value?, reason? }`, and simulates `Promise.all` behavior:

- If **any** result has `status: 'rejected'`, return `'rejected: ' + reason` (use the first rejection found)
- If **all** are fulfilled, return the values joined by commas
- If the array is empty, return an empty string

test_cases.name: Rejection in middle — first one wins

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `asyncSequence(config)` that takes `{ initial, steps }` and applies a sequence of named operations to `initial`, one at a time (like a chain of `await` calls).

Supported operations:
- `'double'`: multiply by 2
- `'addTen'`: add 10
- `'halve'`: divide by 2
- `'negate'`: multiply by -1
- `'square'`: multiply by itself

Return the final result as a number.

Write a function `classifyAsyncPattern(code)` that takes a string snippet of JavaScript code and identifies which async pattern it uses:

- If it contains `await ` → `'async-await'`
- If it contains `.then(` → `'promise-chain'`
- If it contains the word `callback` → `'callback'`
- Otherwise → `'synchronous'`

Check in the order listed above.

Write a function `categorizeModuleSyntax(lines)` that takes an array of code line strings and classifies each line as `'esm'`, `'cjs'`, or `'other'`.

Rules:
- `'esm'` if the line starts with `import` or `export` (after optional whitespace)
- `'cjs'` if the line contains `require(`, `module.exports`, or `exports.`
- `'other'` otherwise

Return the result as an array of labels (String() joins with commas).

test_cases.name: ESM import, plain code, CJS export
ESM import followed by CJS require

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `strictnessScore(options)` that takes a `tsconfig` options object and returns a score from `0` to `3` counting how many of these three key strict options are enabled (truthy):

- `strict`
- `noImplicitAny`
- `strictNullChecks`

Return the count as a number.

Write a function `formatNullable(value)` that handles a union type of `string | number | null | undefined`:

- If `value` is `null` or `undefined` → return `'N/A'`
- If `value` is a number → return it as a string
- If `value` is a string → return it uppercased

This pattern is ubiquitous in TypeScript code with `strictNullChecks` enabled.

Write a function `getInterfaceInfo(obj)` that takes an object matching the shape `{ name: string, age?: number, email?: string }` and returns a formatted string:

`'<name>, <age or "no age">, <email or "no email">'`

This mirrors how TypeScript optional properties (`?`) are handled at runtime: check if the property is `undefined` and substitute a default.

test_cases.name: Name and age provided — email missing
Only name — both optional fields missing

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `getObjectProp(config)` that takes `{ obj, key }` and returns `obj[key]`.

This is the runtime equivalent of TypeScript's `keyof` operator. In TypeScript, this would be typed as `function getObjectProp<T, K extends keyof T>(config: { obj: T, key: K }): T[K]` — but here we test that the runtime behavior is correct.

test_cases.name: Returns zero without treating it as falsy

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `narrowByTag(item)` that computes the area of a shape based on a discriminant `tag` property — this is the runtime implementation of a TypeScript discriminated union.

- `{ tag: 'circle', radius: number }` → area = `Math.PI * radius²`, rounded to 2 decimal places using `toFixed(2)`
- `{ tag: 'rect', width: number, height: number }` → area = `width * height`

For the circle, return the `toFixed(2)` string. For the rect, return the raw number.

Write a function `applyPartial(config)` that takes `{ defaults, overrides, pick }` and returns the value of `pick` from the merged object — simulating `Partial<T>` and object spreading at runtime.

Merge: `{ ...defaults, ...overrides }`, then return the value at `pick`.

Write a function `parseQuerySelector(selector)` that takes a CSS selector string and classifies it as one of: `'id'`, `'class'`, `'attribute'`, `'compound'`, or `'tag'`.

Rules (in order):
1. Starts with `#` → `'id'`
2. Starts with `.` → `'class'`
3. Starts with `[` → `'attribute'`
4. Contains `.`, ` `, `[`, `:`, `>`, `+`, or `~` after the first character → `'compound'`
5. Otherwise → `'tag'`

Write a function `simulateEventBubble(config)` that takes `{ tree, clickTarget, stopAt }` and simulates DOM event bubbling:

- `tree`: ordered array of element names from innermost to outermost (e.g. `['button', 'div', 'body']`)
- `clickTarget`: the element that was clicked (must be in the tree)
- `stopAt`: if not `null`, the element where `stopPropagation()` was called (bubbling stops here, inclusive)

Return the elements the event visited as a comma-joined string.

test_cases.name: Stop at click target — event doesn't bubble
Click starts mid-tree, bubbles to top

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `parseEnvLine(line)` that parses a single `.env` file line:

- Trim the line. If it's empty or starts with `#`, return `'skip'`
- Otherwise, split on the first `=`, trim both sides, and return `'KEY=value'`

This is the core of how `dotenv` works internally.

Write a function `routeRequest(config)` that takes `{ method, path }` and returns the handler name — simulating the routing logic inside a Node.js `http.createServer` handler.

Routing table:
- `GET /health` → `'healthCheck'`
- `GET /users` → `'listUsers'`
- `POST /users` → `'createUser'`
- `GET /users/:id` (path matches `/users/` + digits) → `'getUser'`
- `PUT /users/:id` → `'updateUser'`
- `DELETE /users/:id` → `'deleteUser'`
- Anything else → `'notFound'`

Write a function `parseHttpStatus(code)` that takes an HTTP status code number and returns its category:

- 100–199 → `'informational'`
- 200–299 → `'success'`
- 300–399 → `'redirect'`
- 400–499 → `'client-error'`
- 500–599 → `'server-error'`
- Anything outside these ranges → `'unknown'`

concepts.label: Host Environments
concepts.description: The runtime container that embeds a JS engine and provides platform-specific APIs (browser: DOM/fetch; Node.js: fs/http).

concepts.label: JavaScript Engine
concepts.description: The component (e.g. V8) that implements ECMAScript and executes JavaScript code.

concepts.label: Type Erasure
concepts.description: TypeScript types are stripped at compile time; nothing type-related exists at runtime.

concepts.label: var Hoisting
concepts.description: var declarations are hoisted and initialized to undefined; accessing them before assignment returns undefined, not an error.

concepts.label: Temporal Dead Zone
concepts.description: The period between entering a block scope and a let/const declaration; accessing the variable throws ReferenceError.

concepts.label: Block Scope
concepts.description: let and const are scoped to the nearest enclosing block {}, unlike var which is function-scoped.

concepts.label: Primitive Types
concepts.description: The 8 JavaScript primitives: string, number, bigint, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, object.

concepts.label: typeof Operator
concepts.description: Returns a string indicating the type of an operand; notable quirk: typeof null === 'object'.

concepts.label: Type Coercion
concepts.description: Implicit conversion between types via ==, + with strings, boolean contexts, and arithmetic operators.

concepts.label: Strict Equality (===)
concepts.description: === checks type and value without coercion. Always use === over == to avoid implicit conversion bugs.

concepts.label: Array Methods
concepts.description: map, filter, reduce, find, findIndex, some, every, flat, flatMap — functional array transformation patterns.

concepts.label: Spread & Rest
concepts.description: ... spread expands iterables into function args or array literals; ... rest collects remaining args into an array.

concepts.label: Destructuring
concepts.description: Unpack values from arrays or properties from objects into variables, with rename and default syntax.

concepts.label: Object Spread
concepts.description: { ...obj } creates a shallow copy; later properties override earlier ones — the basis for immutable updates.

concepts.label: Function Forms
concepts.description: Function declarations vs expressions vs arrow functions — hoisting, this, and appropriate use cases.

concepts.label: Hoisting
concepts.description: The JavaScript behavior of moving declarations (var, function) to the top of their scope before execution.

concepts.label: Lexical Scope
concepts.description: Scope is determined at write-time from the source position of code, not at call-time.

concepts.label: Closures
concepts.description: Functions that retain access to variables from their lexical scope after that scope has exited.

concepts.label: Loop Closure Bug
concepts.description: Using var in a loop creates one shared binding; all closures capture the same variable. Fix with let.

concepts.label: this Binding
concepts.description: The value of this is determined by the call site, not the function definition — five binding rules apply.

concepts.label: this Call-Site Rules
concepts.description: Priority order: new > explicit (.call/.apply/.bind) > implicit (obj.method()) > default (undefined/global).

concepts.label: Explicit Binding
concepts.description: call(), apply(), and bind() explicitly set the this value. bind() returns a new function; the others invoke immediately.

concepts.label: Arrow Functions
concepts.description: Arrow functions have no own this — they inherit it lexically from the enclosing scope. Cannot be overridden with call/bind.

concepts.label: Event Loop
concepts.description: The mechanism that picks tasks from the task queue and runs them on the call stack one at a time.

concepts.label: Microtask Queue
concepts.description: Promise callbacks queue as microtasks, which drain entirely after each task before the next macrotask runs.

concepts.label: Promises
concepts.description: Represent the eventual result of an async operation; states: pending, fulfilled, rejected.

concepts.label: Promise Combinators
concepts.description: Promise.all (fail-fast parallel), Promise.allSettled (wait-all parallel), Promise.race, Promise.any.

concepts.label: async / await
concepts.description: Syntactic sugar over Promises. async functions always return a Promise; await pauses until settlement.

concepts.label: ES Modules
concepts.description: The standard import/export module system. Static, tree-shakeable, works in browser and Node.js.

concepts.label: CommonJS vs ESM
concepts.description: CJS uses require/module.exports (dynamic, Node.js legacy). ESM uses import/export (static, modern). They are not directly interoperable.

concepts.label: tsconfig.json
concepts.description: TypeScript compiler configuration: strict, target, lib, module, moduleResolution, noEmit.

concepts.label: Type Inference
concepts.description: TypeScript infers types from context — literal types for const, widened types for let, parameter types must be explicit.

concepts.label: Union Types
concepts.description: string | number — a type that can be one of several types. Requires handling all members for safe use.

concepts.label: strictNullChecks
concepts.description: With strictNullChecks, null and undefined are separate types. Accessing properties on nullable types requires a guard.

concepts.label: Literal Types
concepts.description: Types that accept only specific values: 'pending' | 'complete' | 'failed'. Used for type-safe string constants.

concepts.label: Structural Typing
concepts.description: Type compatibility is based on shape, not name. A type that has at least the required properties is compatible.

concepts.label: Interface vs Type Alias
concepts.description: interface: object shapes, supports extends and declaration merging. type: unions, intersections, utility type expressions.

concepts.label: Generics
concepts.description: Type parameters <T> that are filled in at use-site, enabling type-safe reusable functions and classes.

concepts.label: keyof Operator
concepts.description: keyof T produces a union of T's property names as string literals. Used with generics for type-safe property access.

concepts.label: Utility Types
concepts.description: Built-in type transformers: Partial, Required, Readonly, Record, Pick, Omit, NonNullable, ReturnType, Parameters.

concepts.label: Type Narrowing
concepts.description: TypeScript refines a type within a conditional block based on runtime checks: typeof, instanceof, in, truthiness.

concepts.label: Discriminated Unions
concepts.description: Union types with a shared literal-typed property (discriminant) that enables exhaustive switch/if narrowing.

concepts.label: DOM Model
concepts.description: The tree of nodes representing an HTML document; manipulated via createElement, appendChild, textContent, classList.

concepts.label: DOM Query Selectors
concepts.description: querySelector, querySelectorAll, getElementById — selecting elements with CSS selectors or IDs.

concepts.label: DOM Events
concepts.description: addEventListener, event object, bubbling, stopPropagation, CustomEvent, and removing listeners.

concepts.label: Event Delegation
concepts.description: Attaching one listener to a parent to handle events from children via bubbling — efficient and works for dynamic children.

concepts.label: Node.js Runtime
concepts.description: V8 outside the browser with process, Buffer, and built-in modules (fs, path, os, child_process).

concepts.label: Node.js Built-in Modules
concepts.description: node:fs, node:path, node:url, node:os — core modules available without installation.

concepts.label: Node.js HTTP Server
concepts.description: http.createServer, request/response streams, routing, body parsing, and res.end().

concepts.label: JavaScript History
concepts.description: Created by Brendan Eich in 10 days (1995), Netscape Navigator, standardized as ECMAScript by ECMA International. Went from toy script language to the world's most deployed runtime.

concepts.label: ECMAScript & TC39
concepts.description: ECMAScript is the language spec; TC39 is the committee that evolves it. Yearly releases since ES2015 (ES6). Engines (V8, SpiderMonkey) implement the spec.

concepts.label: TypeScript Origin
concepts.description: Created by Anders Hejlsberg at Microsoft (2012). TypeScript adds a static type system on top of JavaScript to catch bugs at compile time, then erases types to plain JS.

concepts.label: JS Strengths & Weaknesses
concepts.description: Strengths: ubiquitous (browser + server), excellent async model, huge ecosystem. Weaknesses: type coercion gotchas, this binding complexity, legacy baggage from the 10-day origin.

paths.label: Full Course
paths.description: The complete TypeScript & JavaScript curriculum in recommended sequence. Suitable for developers building a solid foundation.

paths.label: Fast Track — TypeScript Focus
paths.description: For developers who already know JavaScript well and want to get productive with TypeScript quickly. Skips JS fundamentals and jumps straight to TypeScript-specific material with just enough JS context.
paths.prerequisites: Comfortable with closures, async/await, array methods, and the event loop

paths.label: Backend Focus — Node.js & TypeScript
paths.description: For developers building APIs and server-side applications with Node.js and TypeScript. Emphasizes async patterns, TypeScript for server code, and Node.js runtime specifics.
paths.notes: M03 and M10 can be read quickly for completeness. M05 (this) is less critical in functional/async Node.js code.

paths.label: Frontend Focus — DOM & React Prep
paths.description: For developers building browser applications. Covers JS fundamentals, async patterns, TypeScript types, and DOM/event APIs needed before learning React or Vue.
paths.notes: M11 (Node.js) can be skipped for pure frontend work but is recommended for full-stack developers.

paths.label: JavaScript Only
paths.description: For developers who need JavaScript proficiency without TypeScript. Covers all JS concepts from variables through Node.js.
paths.notes: M16 (tsconfig) in M07 can be skimmed. TypeScript-specific content in other modules is clearly labeled.
